title: Lists, tuples and slicing

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Lists, tuples and slicing; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapter 5: lists, tuples, sets, dictionaries, looping, and sequence comparison; atomic topic: Lists, tuples and slicing
sources.claim: Python lists are mutable sequences, tuples are immutable sequence values often used for fixed records, and slicing creates a shallow sequence result with normalized bounds.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python lists are mutable sequences, tuples are immutable sequence values often used for fixed records, and slicing creates a shallow sequence result with normalized bounds.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A list stores editable readings, a tuple returns a fixed coordinate pair, and values[:] creates a shallow outer copy before a local reorder. Your goal is to implement sequence transformations while distinguishing mutation, copying and rebinding. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: for, while and loop control; pf data selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python lists are mutable sequences, tuples are immutable sequence values often used for fixed records, and slicing creates a shallow sequence result with normalized bounds. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A tuple can contain mutable objects, slice copies retain nested aliases, negative indices wrap within bounds, repeated-list construction can duplicate one inner reference, and large slices allocate. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Tuple contents are deeply immutable and slicing a nested list recursively copies every contained value.

includes: Use mutable sequences, immutable records and bounded slicing operations.
Implement sequence transformations while distinguishing mutation, copying and rebinding.
excludes: A tuple can contain mutable objects, slice copies retain nested aliases, negative indices wrap within bounds, repeated-list construction can duplicate one inner reference, and large slices allocate.

outcomes: Implement sequence transformations while distinguishing mutation, copying and rebinding.

examples.title: Lists, tuples and slicing: direct language case
examples.situation: A list stores editable readings, a tuple returns a fixed coordinate pair, and values[:] creates a shallow outer copy before a local reorder.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A list stores editable readings, a tuple returns a fixed coordinate pair, and values[:] creates a shallow outer copy before a local reorder.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: List mutation and tuple immutability apply to the outer sequence; slices are shallow copies, so nested mutable objects may remain shared.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because List mutation and tuple immutability apply to the outer sequence; slices are shallow copies, so nested mutable objects may remain shared.

examples.title: Lists, tuples and slicing: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A matrix is built with a comprehension instead of [[0] * cols] * rows so each row is a distinct list.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A matrix is built with a comprehension instead of [[0] * cols] * rows so each row is a distinct list.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A tuple can contain mutable objects, slice copies retain nested aliases, negative indices wrap within bounds, repeated-list construction can duplicate one inner reference, and large slices allocate.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Tuple contents are deeply immutable and slicing a nested list recursively copies every contained value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement sequence transformations while distinguishing mutation, copying and rebinding.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Tuple contents are deeply immutable and slicing a nested list recursively copies every contained value.

analogy.example: a flexible storeroom with ordered shelves, fixed labels, unique membership, lookup indexes, and shared nested containers can introduce Lists, tuples and slicing, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets, aliasing, and text processing
limitations: hashability, shallow copying, Unicode normalization, and object identity require Python's exact data model
